corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

location.sentence: Thus, our MT evaluation system requires two ingredients: 1. a numerical “translation closeness” metric 2. a corpus of good quality human reference trans- lations We fashion our closeness metric after the highly suc- cessful word error rate metric used by the speech recognition community, appropriately modified for multiple reference translations and allowing for le- gitimate differences in word choice and word or- der.

context_window.previous: To judge the quality of a machine translation, one measures its closeness to one or more reference human translations accord- ing to a numerical metric.

context_window.next: The main idea is to use a weighted average of variable length phrase matches against the reference translations.

top_candidates.sentence: BLEU's strength is that it correlates highly with human judg- Crossing this chasm for Chinese-English translation appears to be a significant challenge for the current state-of-the-art systems. ments by averaging out individual sentence judgment errors over a test corpus rather than attempting to divine the exact human judgment for every sentence: quantity leads to quality.

Thus, one must be cautious making even "rough" comparisons on evaluations with different numbers of reference translations: on a test corpus of about 500 sentences (40 general news stories), a human translator scored 0.3468 against four references and scored 0.2571 against two references.
Table shows the BLEU scores of the 5 systems against two references on this test corpus.
Table shows the BLEU scores of the 5 systems against two references on this test corpus.

Hence, several questions arise: • Is the difference in BLEU metric reliable? • What is the variance of the BLEU score? • If we were to pick another random set of 500 sentences, would we still judge S3 to be better than S2?
To answer these questions, we divided the test corpus into 20 blocks of 25 sentences each, and computed the BLEU metric on these blocks individually.
To answer these questions, we divided the test corpus into 20 blocks of 25 sentences each, and computed the BLEU metric on these blocks individually.
We thus have 20 samples of the BLEU metric for each system.
Note that the numbers in Table are the BLEU metric on an aggregate of 500 sentences, but the means in Table are averages of the BLEU metric on aggregates of 25 sentences.
The reported variance on 25-sentence blocks serves as an upper bound to the variance of sizeable test sets like the 500 sentence corpus.
We simulated a single-reference test corpus by randomly selecting one of the 4 reference translations as the single reference for each of the 40 stories.
This outcome suggests that we may use a big test corpus with a single reference translation, provided that the translations are not all from the same translator.
The humans judged our 5 standard systems on a Chinese sentence subset extracted at random from our 500 sentence test corpus.
Figure shows a linear regression of the monolingual group scores as a function of the BLEU score over two reference translations for the 5 systems.
The high correlation coefficient of 0.99 indicates that BLEU tracks human judgment well.
Particularly interesting is how well BLEU distinguishes between S2 and S3 which are quite close.

Figure 6: BLEU predicts Bilingual Judgments We now take the worst system as a reference point and compare the BLEU scores with the human judg-ment scores of the remaining systems relative to the worst system.
We took the BLEU, monolingual group, and bilingual group scores for the 5 systems and linearly normalized them by their corresponding range (the maximum and minimum score across the 5 systems).
This figure illustrates the high correlation between the BLEU score and the monolingual group.
Of particular interest is the accuracy of BLEU's estimate of the small difference between S2 and S3 and the larger difference between S3 and H1.
Of particular interest is the accuracy of BLEU's estimate of the small difference between S2 and S3 and the larger difference between S3 and H1.
The BLEU metric ranges from 0 to 1.

We propose a method of automatic machine translation evaluation that is quick, inexpensive, and language-independent, that correlates highly with human evaluation, and that has little marginal cost per run.
We present this method as an automated understudy to skilled human judges which substitutes for them when there is need for quick or frequent evaluations.
We believe that BLEU will accelerate the MT R&D cycle by allowing researchers to rapidly home in on effective modeling ideas.
